learning_objectives: Define healthcare quality using the IOM six aims
Trace the history of the quality movement from Codman to modern measurement
Understand the role of informatics in enabling quality improvement
readings: McLaughlin and Kaluzny's Continuous Quality Improvement in Health Care Ch. 1-2
IOM Crossing the Quality Chasm Executive Summary

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare quality management has evolved from a retrospective chart review activity to a data-driven, real-time discipline. Informatics professionals are essential to making quality measurement and improvement work at scale.
key_concepts: IOM six aims: safe, effective, patient-centered, timely, efficient, equitable
History: Codman (outcomes tracking), Donabedian (structure-process-outcome), IOM reports
Quality measurement: structure, process, and outcome measures
Regulatory drivers: CMS value-based programs, accreditation (Joint Commission, NCQA)
The informatics role: data capture, measure calculation, reporting, visualization
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the CMS Hospital Compare website analyzing quality star ratings and measure performance for three hospitals

review.value: Safe (avoid harm), Effective (evidence-based), Patient-centered (respect preferences), Timely (reduce waits), Efficient (avoid waste), Equitable (consistent regardless of demographics)
Framework linking Structure (resources/organization) to Process (care activities) to Outcomes (health results)

class_schedule.topic: Quality Measurement and Clinical Quality Measures
learning_objectives: Describe the anatomy of a clinical quality measure (CQM)
Differentiate between eCQMs, HEDIS, and claims-based measures
Implement measure logic using EHR data

lecture_notes.introduction: Clinical quality measures translate care standards into quantifiable metrics. Understanding how they are specified and calculated is essential for informatics professionals supporting quality programs.
key_concepts: CQM anatomy: initial population, denominator, denominator exclusions, numerator, denominator exceptions
eCQMs: measures calculated from EHR data using CQL (Clinical Quality Language)
HEDIS: managed care quality measures used by health plans
Claims-based measures: calculated from billing data (e.g., readmission rates)
Measure stewards: CMS, NCQA, AMA-PCPI, The Joint Commission
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the specification for CMS68v12 (Documentation of Current Medications) and trace its logic through EHR data elements
setup_requirements: Sample EHR data for measure calculation

review.value: Initial population (who is eligible), denominator (who should receive care), numerator (who received it), exclusions/exceptions (who is appropriately excluded)
Clinical Quality Language: HL7 standard for expressing measure logic in a computable, shareable format

learning_objectives: Apply Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) cycles to quality improvement projects
Compare Lean, Six Sigma, and Model for Improvement methodologies
Design a QI project using structured methodology

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality measurement identifies problems; quality improvement methodologies provide structured approaches to solving them. The informatics professional must understand both measurement and improvement.
key_concepts: Model for Improvement: three questions (aim, measures, changes) + PDSA cycles
Lean healthcare: value stream mapping, waste reduction (8 wastes of healthcare)
Six Sigma DMAIC: Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control
Root cause analysis: fishbone diagrams, 5 Whys, process mapping
Data-driven QI: using SPC charts to track improvement over time
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply PDSA methodology to reduce emergency department door-to-provider time, including data collection plan and SPC chart design

review.value: Plan (design the test), Do (carry out the test), Study (analyze results), Act (adopt, adapt, or abandon the change)
Lean tool visualizing every step in a process, identifying value-added vs. non-value-added activities to eliminate waste

class_schedule.topic: Patient Safety and Adverse Event Reporting
learning_objectives: Describe patient safety event classification and reporting systems
Understand root cause analysis for serious safety events
Evaluate the role of health IT in patient safety and iatrogenic harm

lecture_notes.introduction: Patient safety is the foundation of quality. Understanding how adverse events are identified, classified, reported, and analyzed is essential, especially as health IT can both prevent and cause safety events.
key_concepts: Patient safety event taxonomy: near miss, adverse event, sentinel event, never event
Reporting systems: internal event reporting, PSOs (Patient Safety Organizations), FDA MedWatch
Root cause analysis (RCA) methodology for sentinel events
Just culture: distinguishing human error, at-risk behavior, and reckless behavior
Health IT safety: EHR-related errors, alert fatigue, wrong-patient orders
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a mock RCA for a wrong-site surgery event using the ECRI RCA framework, identifying system factors and informatics interventions

review.value: Organizational approach that distinguishes between blameless human error (console), risky behavior (coach), and reckless behavior (punish) to encourage reporting
An unexpected occurrence involving death or serious physical/psychological injury, requiring immediate investigation and corrective action

class_schedule.topic: Value-Based Quality Programs and Public Reporting
learning_objectives: Analyze CMS value-based purchasing programs and their quality metrics
Understand the informatics infrastructure required for quality reporting
Evaluate the impact of public reporting on quality improvement
readings: CMS Hospital Value-Based Purchasing Program Fact Sheet

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality measurement now directly impacts hospital revenue through value-based purchasing. The informatics infrastructure supporting these programs must be accurate, timely, and reliable.
key_concepts: CMS Hospital VBP: domains (safety, clinical care, patient experience, efficiency)
Hospital-Acquired Condition Reduction Program (HACRP)
Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program (HRRP)
MIPS (Merit-based Incentive Payment System) for clinicians
Quality data infrastructure: extraction, validation, submission, monitoring
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculate a hospital's VBP total performance score using sample data and determine the payment adjustment

review.value: CMS program adjusting Medicare payments (up to +/- 2%) based on hospital performance on quality measures relative to achievement and improvement benchmarks
Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program: reduces payments (up to 3%) for hospitals with excess readmissions for targeted conditions

learning_objectives: Analyze quality measure performance across demographic groups
Understand CMS health equity quality initiatives
Design quality improvement interventions targeting disparities

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality without equity is incomplete. Aggregate quality metrics can mask significant disparities. Stratifying measures by race, ethnicity, language, and socioeconomic status reveals where targeted improvement is needed.
key_concepts: Health equity defined: the attainment of the highest level of health for all people
Quality measure stratification by race, ethnicity, language, disability, SOGI
CMS health equity provisions: Hospital Equity Score, equity-adjusted quality measures
Social determinants screening and documentation in quality programs
Designing equity-focused QI interventions: community health workers, navigation, language access
lecture_notes.demonstration: Stratify hospital readmission rates by race and insurance type, identify disparities, and design targeted interventions

review.value: Calculating quality metrics separately for demographic subgroups to reveal disparities hidden in aggregate performance
CMS metric assessing hospital performance on equity through data collection, quality disparities, and community engagement

questions.question: Describe the components of an electronic Clinical Quality Measure (eCQM). Using the example of a diabetes A1c control measure, define the initial population, denominator, numerator, and at least one denominator exclusion.
questions.answer: eCQM components for diabetes A1c control: Initial population: patients aged 18-75 with at least one encounter during the measurement period. Denominator: patients from the initial population with a diagnosis of Type 1 or Type 2 diabetes. Numerator: patients from the denominator with most recent A1c result <9.0% (controlled). Denominator exclusion: patients in hospice care during the measurement period, or patients with end-stage renal disease, since A1c is unreliable in these populations. The measure is calculated as numerator/denominator, expressed as a percentage of diabetic patients with controlled A1c.

rubric.key: Valid denominator exclusion with clinical rationale

questions.question: Compare the PDSA cycle (Model for Improvement) and Six Sigma DMAIC methodology. When would you recommend each approach for a healthcare quality improvement project?
questions.answer: PDSA (Model for Improvement) uses rapid, small-scale testing cycles. It starts with three questions: What are we trying to accomplish? How will we know that a change is an improvement? What changes can we make? Then tests changes through iterative Plan-Do-Study-Act cycles. Best for: frontline clinical teams with practical problems needing quick tests of change (e.g., reducing patient falls on a single unit). Six Sigma DMAIC is a data-intensive, statistical methodology: Define the problem, Measure current performance, Analyze root causes using statistical tools, Improve with validated solutions, Control to sustain gains. Best for: complex, organization-wide problems requiring rigorous statistical analysis to identify root causes (e.g., reducing hospital-wide surgical site infections where multiple variables contribute).

rubric.key: Appropriate use case recommendation for each
Understanding of when each is most effective

questions.question: Explain the 'just culture' framework and provide an example of each category (human error, at-risk behavior, reckless behavior) in a clinical informatics context.
questions.answer: Just culture distinguishes between three types of behavior to determine appropriate response: (1) Human error (console): a nurse selects the wrong patient from a dropdown list in the EHR because two patients have similar names and the interface does not prominently display distinguishing information. This is an inadvertent slip in a poorly designed system. (2) At-risk behavior (coach): a physician routinely overrides drug interaction alerts without reading them because 'they are always irrelevant.' The physician has drifted into risky behavior that feels justified but increases patient risk. Response: coaching on alert review and reporting alert fatigue issues to the CDS committee. (3) Reckless behavior (punish): an IT administrator disables audit logging on the EHR to improve system performance, knowing this violates HIPAA and removes the ability to detect unauthorized access. This is a conscious, unjustifiable choice.

rubric.key: Correct framework description with three categories
Valid informatics-specific example for each category

questions.question: A hospital's 30-day readmission rate for heart failure is 25%, above the CMS national average of 21.5%. Calculate the financial impact under the Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program (assuming $50M in base DRG payments and a maximum 3% penalty), and outline an informatics-supported quality improvement approach.
questions.answer: Financial impact: If the hospital's excess readmission ratio leads to the maximum penalty, the reduction would be $50M x 0.03 = $1.5M in annual Medicare payment reduction. The actual penalty depends on the excess readmission ratio calculation. QI approach: (1) Stratify readmissions by root cause using EHR and claims data (medication non-adherence, lack of follow-up, social factors). (2) Implement predictive model identifying high-risk patients at admission for targeted interventions. (3) Deploy automated post-discharge follow-up system (patient portal messages, telehealth check-ins at 48-72 hours). (4) Build a real-time readmission tracking dashboard for the care management team. (5) Implement medication reconciliation alerts at discharge and a pharmacist-reviewed discharge medication list. (6) Monitor improvement using SPC charts and adjust interventions based on data.

major_assignments.title: Quality Improvement Project with Informatics Integration
major_assignments.description: Design and present a comprehensive quality improvement project for a healthcare organization. The project must use structured QI methodology, leverage informatics tools for measurement and improvement, and include an equity analysis.
objectives: Apply structured QI methodology (PDSA or DMAIC) to a real quality problem
Design informatics-enabled measurement and monitoring systems
Incorporate health equity analysis into quality improvement
Develop a compelling business case for the QI project
deliverables: QI project charter and aim statement
Current state analysis with data (10-12 pages)
Intervention design with informatics tools specified

criteria: Appropriate QI methodology selected and applied
Clear aim statement with measurable goals
Equity metrics included in evaluation plan
Strong informatics integration for measurement and improvement
Meaningful equity analysis with targeted interventions

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of quality concepts, methodologies, and program knowledge

class_participation.description: Active engagement in QI exercises, case studies, and group activities

major_assignments.description: Quality improvement project with informatics integration

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of quality measurement and improvement methodologies

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including patient safety, VBP programs, and health equity

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. QI project work sessions are mandatory. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 10% per day. QI project milestones must be met for iterative feedback.
course_policies.collaboration: QI methodology exercises are collaborative. Written project deliverables must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All analysis must use properly cited data sources. Quality data from real organizations must be properly de-identified.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with internet access, spreadsheet software, SPC charting tool (QI Macros or free alternative), access to CMS quality data.
